1:
"Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free, and be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage." The witnesses note the call to stand fast in Christian liberty. On "stand fast in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free," the grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's ringing exhortation, the conclusion of the allegory: "stand fast (stand firm, hold your ground) in the liberty (freedom from the law's yoke) wherewith Christ hath made us free" — the freedom Christ purchased is to be guarded.

On "be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage," the witnesses note the warning. Barnes and Calvin note the danger: "be not entangled (held fast, ensnared) again with the yoke of bondage" — do not submit once more to the slavery of the law (circumcision and ceremonies) from which Christ freed them.

The lutheran Luther notes the doctrine: Christian liberty is freedom from the law, sin, and death as the ground of righteousness; to return to the law is to take up again an intolerable yoke. The verse displays the call to stand fast in Christian liberty.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's ringing charge — "stand fast (hold firm) in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free," the freedom Christ purchased to be guarded (Barnes, Robertson); the warning "be not entangled (ensnared) again with the yoke of bondage," not to return to the slavery of the law (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Christian liberty is freedom from the law as the ground of righteousness. The verse displays standing fast in liberty. The verse marks the charge: stand fast. The verse displays the liberty: Christ's gift. The verse displays the warning: not the yoke again. The verse displays the bondage: of the law. The charge displays the believer guarding Christ's freedom. The verse displays the standing: firm. The verse displays the freedom: from the law. The verse displays the yoke: refused. The call to stand fast in Christian liberty displays the great conclusion of Paul's argument: the freedom Christ purchased for believers is to be firmly held, and they are not to be ensnared again in the yoke of legal bondage from which he set them free. The verse displays the believer's liberty, defended against circumcision (vv.2-6).
2:
"Behold, I Paul say unto you, that if ye be circumcised, Christ shall profit you nothing." The brief verse notes Paul's solemn warning that circumcision nullifies Christ. The grammatical voices note the emphasis. Barnes and Robertson note Paul speaks with the full weight of his apostolic authority: "behold, I Paul say unto you" — a solemn personal asseveration — "that if ye be circumcised (submit to it as necessary to justification), Christ shall profit you nothing."

On "Christ shall profit you nothing," the witnesses note the incompatibility. Barnes and Calvin note that to seek justification by circumcision (and so by the law) is to renounce Christ — for the two ways of justification cannot be combined; whoever trusts the law gains nothing from Christ.

The lutheran Luther notes the sharpness: Paul will not allow the smallest mingling of law and gospel as the ground of righteousness; to add circumcision is to lose Christ entirely. The verse displays Paul's solemn warning that circumcision nullifies Christ.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul speaks with full apostolic weight — "behold, I Paul say unto you," a solemn asseveration — "that if ye be circumcised (as necessary to justification), Christ shall profit you nothing" (Barnes, Robertson); to seek justification by circumcision is to renounce Christ, the two ways being incompatible (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Paul allows no mingling of law and gospel. The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the asseveration: I Paul say. The verse displays the condition: if circumcised. The verse displays the loss: Christ profits nothing. The verse displays the incompatibility: law or Christ. The warning displays circumcision nullifying the benefit of Christ. The verse displays the solemnity: of Paul. The verse displays the circumcision: as righteousness. The verse displays the Christ: profiting nothing. Paul's solemn warning that circumcision nullifies Christ displays the absolute incompatibility of law and grace as grounds of justification: with his full apostolic authority Paul declares that whoever submits to circumcision as necessary to be justified renounces Christ and gains nothing from him. The verse displays the either/or of justification, pressed by the law's whole demand (v.3).
3:
"For I testify again to every man that is circumcised, that he is a debtor to do the whole law." The brief verse notes that circumcision binds one to keep the whole law. The grammatical voices note the obligation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's testimony: "every man that is circumcised (as a means of justification) is a debtor (bound, obligated) to do the whole law" — to accept circumcision on that ground is to assume the obligation to keep the entire Mosaic law.

On "the whole law," the witnesses note the burden. Barnes and Calvin note the crushing consequence: one who would be justified by the law must keep all of it perfectly — not part, but the whole; and since none can, this way leads only to the curse.

The lutheran Luther notes the trap: the Judaizers offered circumcision as a small thing, but it binds the conscience to the impossible burden of the entire law; there is no partial keeping. The verse displays that circumcision binds one to keep the whole law.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul testifies that "every man that is circumcised (as a means of justification) is a debtor to do the whole law," assuming the obligation to keep the entire Mosaic law (Barnes, Robertson); the crushing consequence that he must keep all perfectly, and since none can, this leads to the curse (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes circumcision binds the conscience to the impossible burden of the whole law. The verse displays the whole-law obligation. The verse marks the testimony: a debtor to the whole law. The verse displays the obligation: keep all. The verse displays the burden: the entire law. The verse displays the consequence: the curse. The obligation displays circumcision binding to the impossible whole law. The verse displays the circumcision: binding. The verse displays the debtor: to the law. The verse displays the whole: required. That circumcision binds one to keep the whole law displays the crushing logic of legalism: to accept circumcision as a means of justification is to become a debtor to the entire Mosaic law — which must be kept whole and perfectly, a burden no one can bear, leading only to condemnation. The verse displays the law's total demand, fallen from grace in the attempt (v.4).
4:
"Christ is become of no effect unto you, whosoever of you are justified by the law; ye are fallen from grace." The witnesses note Christ made void and the fall from grace — the great perseverance crux. On "Christ is become of no effect... whosoever are justified by the law," the grammatical voices note the severance. Barnes and Robertson note that those who "would be justified by the law" are "severed from Christ" (made void of him, abolished from him) — to seek righteousness by law is to render Christ useless to oneself, leaving the sphere of grace for the sphere of law.

On "ye are fallen from grace," interpreters divide over its bearing on perseverance. The grammatical-historical Barnes and Robertson, with the Reformed Calvin, hold the statement is conditional and hypothetical — "grace" meaning the system of justification by grace, not personal saving grace; since Paul elsewhere denies anyone is justified by the law (Romans 3:20), the verse does not prove that a true believer can finally apostatize, but that to adopt the legal scheme is to reject the gospel of grace. The wesleyan-arminian Clarke and Wesley take it as a real falling — those who readopt the law have apostatized from the gospel and lost the grace they had received (Clarke: "whether some of them ever rose again is more than we can tell").

The witnesses note the warning: to mingle law with Christ is to renounce both Christ and grace. The verse displays Christ made void and the fall from grace.

The witnesses agree the severance: those who would "be justified by the law" are cut off from Christ, rendering him of no effect, having left the sphere of grace for that of law (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin). But "ye are fallen from grace" divides them on perseverance: Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin read it as conditional — "grace" being the gospel system, not personal saving grace, so (since none is justified by law) it does not prove a true believer can finally fall, but that to adopt legalism is to reject the gospel; Clarke and Wesley read it as a real falling from the saving grace once received. The verse displays the fall from grace. The verse marks the severance: Christ of no effect. The verse displays the debate: hypothetical or real apostasy. The verse displays the grace: the system or the personal. The verse displays the warning: law renounces Christ. The crux displays the fall from grace read two ways. The verse displays the Christ: made void. The verse displays the falling: from grace. The verse displays the law: the cause. Christ made void and the fall from grace display both the severity of the warning and a great divide: to seek justification by law severs one from Christ and falls from grace — read by Barnes and Calvin as the hypothetical renouncing of the gospel system (no proof a true believer apostatizes), and by Clarke and Wesley as a real falling from saving grace. The verse displays the renouncing of grace, against which the Spirit waits in hope (v.5).
5:
"For we through the Spirit wait for the hope of righteousness by faith." The brief verse notes believers waiting through the Spirit for the hope of righteousness by faith. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul sets the true way over against the legal: "we (true believers) through the Spirit (not the flesh or the law) wait for (eagerly expect) the hope of righteousness by faith" — the full hope that righteousness by faith secures.

On "the hope of righteousness," the witnesses note the object. Barnes and Calvin note "the hope of righteousness" — either the hoped-for blessings that flow from being justified (the final glory), or righteousness itself as the object of hope; believers, already justified by faith, await the consummation of that hope.

The lutheran Luther notes the manner: the Christian life is one of waiting in hope, by the Spirit and through faith — not earning by works, but expecting the promised righteousness and glory. The verse displays believers waiting through the Spirit for the hope of righteousness by faith.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul sets the true way over against the legal — "we through the Spirit (not the flesh or law) wait for (eagerly expect) the hope of righteousness by faith" (Barnes, Robertson); "the hope of righteousness" being the hoped-for blessings flowing from justification, or righteousness as the object of hope, awaited by faith (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the Christian waits in hope by the Spirit and faith, not earning by works. The verse displays waiting in hope. The verse marks the means: through the Spirit. The verse displays the manner: by faith. The verse displays the object: the hope of righteousness. The verse displays the contrast: not the law. The waiting displays believers expecting righteousness by faith. The verse displays the Spirit: the means. The verse displays the hope: of righteousness. The verse displays the faith: the way. Believers waiting through the Spirit for the hope of righteousness by faith displays the true posture of the justified: over against those who seek righteousness by the law, true believers, by the Spirit and through faith, eagerly await the full hope that righteousness by faith secures. The verse displays the life of faith and hope, in which love is what avails (v.6).
6:
"For in Jesus Christ neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision; but faith which worketh by love." The witnesses note that in Christ only faith working by love avails. On "neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision," the grammatical voices note the indifference of the externals. Barnes and Robertson note that "in Jesus Christ" (in the Christian state, in the matter of justification) neither circumcision nor uncircumcision has any value — the outward rite, kept or unkept, contributes nothing to one's standing with God.

On "faith which worketh by love," the witnesses note what does avail. Barnes and Calvin note the one thing that counts: "faith which worketh (is energized, operative) by love" — a living faith that expresses itself in love; justifying faith is never idle but works through love. The Reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical guard the doctrine: love is the fruit and evidence of faith, not the ground of justification — faith alone justifies, but the faith that justifies is a working, loving faith (against the reading that faith is "formed" or made justifying by love).

The lutheran Luther notes the balance: faith alone justifies, yet true faith is not dead but bears fruit in love; works and love follow faith as its necessary effect, not its cause. The verse displays that in Christ only faith working by love avails.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: "in Jesus Christ neither circumcision nor uncircumcision availeth any thing" — the outward rite contributes nothing to one's standing with God (Barnes, Robertson); the one thing that counts is "faith which worketh (is operative) by love," a living faith expressing itself in love (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin and Luther guard that love is the fruit and evidence of faith, not the ground of justification — faith alone justifies, but justifying faith is never dead, working through love (against the "faith formed by love" reading). The verse displays faith working by love. The verse marks the indifference: circumcision avails nothing. The verse displays what avails: faith working by love. The verse displays the doctrine: love the fruit, not the ground. The verse displays the life: faith never idle. The faith displays the living faith that works through love. The verse displays the externals: worthless. The verse displays the faith: operative. The verse displays the love: its fruit. That in Christ only faith working by love avails displays the heart of the gospel life: outward rites count for nothing in justification, and the one thing that avails is a living faith that works through love — love being the fruit and evidence of justifying faith, not its ground. The verse displays the working faith, hindered in those who left the truth (v.7).
7:
"Ye did run well; who did hinder you that ye should not obey the truth?" The brief verse notes Paul asking who hindered their good progress. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note the racing metaphor: "ye did run well" — the Galatians had begun the Christian course with vigor and good progress — "who did hinder (cut in on, impede, as a runner cutting across the track) you that ye should not obey the truth?"

On the question, the witnesses note the rebuke and the culprits. Barnes and Henry note Paul's pointed question implies that someone had wrongfully interfered — the false teachers — who had blocked their progress and turned them from obedience to the truth of the gospel.

The lutheran Luther notes the grief: it pains the apostle that those who ran so well should be tripped up by the Judaizers; good beginnings are no security against being hindered. The verse displays Paul asking who hindered their good progress.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the racing metaphor — "ye did run well" (the Galatians began the Christian course with vigor) — "who did hinder (cut in on, impede) you that ye should not obey the truth?" (Barnes, Robertson); the pointed question implying the false teachers had wrongfully interfered, blocking their progress (Barnes, Henry). Luther notes it grieves Paul that good runners were tripped by the Judaizers. The verse displays the question of hindrance. The verse marks the figure: ye did run well. The verse displays the question: who hindered you? The verse displays the culprits: the false teachers. The verse displays the grief: good runners tripped. The question displays the Galatians' good course impeded. The verse displays the running: well. The verse displays the hindrance: by others. The verse displays the truth: disobeyed. Paul asking who hindered their good progress displays his grieved astonishment: the Galatians had run the Christian race well, and he asks pointedly who cut across their course and turned them from obeying the truth — the false teachers who had tripped them up. The verse displays the hindrance of their course, traced not to God (v.8).
8:
"This persuasion cometh not of him that calleth you." The brief verse notes that their false persuasion is not from God who calls them. The grammatical voices note the source denied. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares that "this persuasion (the influence that turned them, the new conviction toward the law) cometh not of him that calleth you" — it does not come from God, who had called them to the gospel of grace.

On "him that calleth you," the witnesses note God's call. Barnes and Calvin note that God, the author of their calling to liberty and faith, is not the source of this legalistic persuasion; it comes from elsewhere — from the false teachers, and ultimately from the enemy.

The lutheran Luther notes the test: whatever turns the soul from the gospel of grace is not of God, however plausible; the call of God is always to Christ and freedom, never to the bondage of the law. The verse displays that their false persuasion is not from God.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declares "this persuasion (the influence toward the law) cometh not of him that calleth you" — not from God, who called them to the gospel of grace (Barnes, Robertson); God, the author of their calling to liberty, is not the source of this legalistic persuasion, which comes from the false teachers (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes whatever turns the soul from grace is not of God. The verse displays the source denied. The verse marks the persuasion: toward the law. The verse displays the denial: not of God. The verse displays the call: to grace. The verse displays the source: the false teachers. The denial displays the legalistic persuasion not from God. The verse displays the persuasion: false. The verse displays the caller: God. The verse displays the disowning: of it. That their false persuasion is not from God who calls them displays the test of its origin: the influence that turned the Galatians toward the law did not come from God, who had called them to the gospel of grace and liberty, but from the false teachers — for God never calls the soul into bondage. The verse displays the alien source of the error, whose small beginning spreads (v.9).
9:
"A little leaven leaveneth the whole lump." The brief verse notes the proverb that a little error corrupts the whole. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul cites the proverb (as in 1 Corinthians 5:6): "a little leaven leaveneth (ferments, pervades) the whole lump" — a small amount of leaven spreads through and transforms the entire batch of dough.

On the application, the witnesses note the danger. Barnes and Calvin note the warning: a little false doctrine, like a little leaven, will spread and corrupt the whole church; the small beginning of the Judaizing error, if tolerated, would pervade and ruin the whole body of believers.

The lutheran Luther notes the urgency: no error in the doctrine of justification is small or harmless; the least corruption of the gospel, like leaven, works through the whole and must be purged. The verse displays the proverb that a little error corrupts the whole.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul cites the proverb (1 Corinthians 5:6) — "a little leaven leaveneth (ferments, pervades) the whole lump," a small amount spreading through the whole batch (Barnes, Robertson); the warning that a little false doctrine, like leaven, will corrupt the whole church if tolerated (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes no error in justification is small; the least corruption works through the whole. The verse displays the leaven proverb. The verse marks the figure: a little leaven. The verse displays the spread: the whole lump. The verse displays the danger: error pervading. The verse displays the urgency: purge it. The proverb displays a small error corrupting the whole body. The verse displays the leaven: little. The verse displays the lump: all. The verse displays the corruption: spreading. The proverb that a little error corrupts the whole displays the peril of tolerating false doctrine: as a little leaven ferments the whole lump, so the small beginning of the Judaizing error, if left unchecked, will spread through and corrupt the entire church. The verse displays the spreading danger, against which Paul has confidence (v.10). The proverb carries a sober lesson for the church of every age, that error in the foundation of the faith is never to be tolerated as a small or private matter, for like leaven hidden in the dough it works silently and surely until the whole mass is corrupted, so that the apostle would have the Galatians purge it out before it should spread beyond recovery.
10:
"I have confidence in you through the Lord, that ye will be none otherwise minded: but he that troubleth you shall bear his judgment, whosoever he be." The brief verse notes Paul's confidence in them and warning to their troubler. The grammatical voices note the hope. Barnes and Robertson note Paul tempers his rebuke with hope: "I have confidence in you through the Lord (trusting in the Lord's working in them) that ye will be none otherwise minded" — that they will return to the truth and not adopt the error.

On "he that troubleth you shall bear his judgment," the witnesses note the warning. Barnes and Calvin note the threat against the false teacher: "he that troubleth (unsettles, disturbs) you shall bear his judgment (the penalty, the condemnation), whosoever he be" — however prominent, the troubler will answer to God.

The lutheran Luther notes the balance: Paul hopes the best for the deceived sheep but pronounces judgment on the deceiver; mercy for the led-astray, severity for the leader. The verse displays Paul's confidence in them and warning to their troubler.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul tempers rebuke with hope — "I have confidence in you through the Lord that ye will be none otherwise minded," trusting they will return to the truth (Barnes, Robertson); the warning that "he that troubleth (unsettles) you shall bear his judgment, whosoever he be," the troubler answering to God however prominent (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes mercy for the deceived, severity for the deceiver. The verse displays confidence and warning. The verse marks the hope: none otherwise minded. The verse displays the trust: through the Lord. The verse displays the warning: bear his judgment. The verse displays the troubler: whosoever. The confidence displays Paul hoping for the sheep, warning the deceiver. The verse displays the confidence: in them. The verse displays the judgment: on the troubler. The verse displays the balance: mercy and severity. Paul's confidence in them and warning to their troubler displays his blended hope and severity: trusting in the Lord that the Galatians will return to the truth, he yet pronounces that the one troubling them — whoever he may be — will bear God's judgment. The verse displays the hope for the misled and threat for the misleader, with a personal vindication (v.11).
11:
"And I, brethren, if I yet preach circumcision, why do I yet suffer persecution? then is the offence of the cross ceased." The brief verse notes Paul's persecution proving he no longer preaches circumcision. The grammatical voices note the refutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul rebuts a slander (that he himself still preached circumcision): "if I yet preach circumcision, why do I yet suffer persecution?" — his continued persecution by the Jews proves he does not, for they would not persecute one who upheld the law.

On "then is the offence of the cross ceased," the witnesses note the stumbling-block. Barnes and Calvin note that if Paul preached circumcision, "the offence (skandalon, stumbling-block) of the cross" would have "ceased" — the cross is an offence to the Jews precisely because it sets aside the law as the way of salvation; were Paul to preach the law, the offence would vanish, and so would his persecution.

The lutheran Luther notes the principle: the preaching of the cross, of free justification apart from the law, always gives offence and draws persecution; to remove the offence is to corrupt the gospel. The verse displays Paul's persecution proving he no longer preaches circumcision.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul rebuts the slander that he preached circumcision — "if I yet preach circumcision, why do I yet suffer persecution?" his persecution by the Jews proving he does not (Barnes, Robertson); for then "the offence (stumbling-block) of the cross" would cease, the cross offending the Jews precisely because it sets aside the law (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the preaching of the cross always gives offence. The verse displays the offence of the cross. The verse marks the refutation: why persecuted? The verse displays the proof: he preaches not circumcision. The verse displays the offence: of the cross. The verse displays the principle: the cross offends. The persecution displays Paul not preaching the law. The verse displays the circumcision: not preached. The verse displays the persecution: continuing. The verse displays the cross: an offence. Paul's persecution proving he no longer preaches circumcision displays his refutation of the slander: if he still upheld the law, the Jews would not persecute him, for the offence of the cross — which sets aside the law as the way of salvation — would have ceased; his continued suffering proves his fidelity to the gospel. The verse displays the offence of the cross, sharpened against the troublers (v.12).
12:
"I would they were even cut off which trouble you." The brief verse notes Paul's sharp wish concerning the troublers. The grammatical voices note the strong expression. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's vehement words against the false teachers: "I would they were even cut off (apokopsontai) which trouble you" — most take it as a sharp play on circumcision, wishing that those who press cutting (circumcision) would go further and cut themselves off entirely (a stern, ironic word of indignation against the troublers).

On the sense, the witnesses note the alternatives. Barnes and Calvin note the readings: some take it that Paul wishes them "cut off (excommunicated, removed)" from the church; others (more literally) that they would mutilate themselves — but all agree it is an expression of strong indignation against those who unsettle the church.

The lutheran Luther notes the zeal: Paul's burning concern for the gospel and the souls of the Galatians makes him speak with vehemence against the deceivers; holy zeal does not mince words against error. The verse displays Paul's sharp wish concerning the troublers.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's vehement words — "I would they were even cut off (apokopsontai) which trouble you," most taking it as a sharp play on circumcision (that those pressing cutting would cut themselves off entirely), a stern word of indignation (Barnes, Robertson); some read it as a wish they be cut off (excommunicated) from the church, all agreeing it expresses strong indignation (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Paul's holy zeal against the deceivers. The verse displays the sharp wish. The verse marks the words: cut off. The verse displays the play: on circumcision. The verse displays the indignation: against troublers. The verse displays the zeal: for the gospel. The wish displays Paul's vehemence against the deceivers. The verse displays the troublers: condemned. The verse displays the cutting: ironic. The verse displays the zeal: burning. Paul's sharp wish concerning the troublers displays the heat of his indignation: with a stern play on their pressing of circumcision, he wishes that those who unsettle the Galatians would cut themselves off entirely — a vehement word, born of holy zeal for the gospel and the souls of the deceived. The verse displays the apostle's zeal against error, turning to the right use of liberty (v.13).
13:
"For, brethren, ye have been called unto liberty; only use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh, but by love serve one another." The witnesses note liberty not to be abused but used in loving service. On "ye have been called unto liberty," the grammatical voices note the calling. Barnes and Robertson note Paul affirms the Galatians' calling: they "have been called unto liberty" — freedom from the law's yoke; but this liberty has limits and a right use.

On "use not liberty for an occasion to the flesh," the witnesses note the abuse to avoid. Barnes and Calvin note the warning: liberty is not to be made "an occasion (a pretext, a base of operations) to the flesh" — Christian freedom is not license to sin; it must not be turned into an excuse for fleshly indulgence.

On "by love serve one another," the puritan Henry and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the right use: "by love serve (be slaves to, douleuete) one another" — the paradox that the free are to make themselves servants of each other through love; true liberty expresses itself in mutual, loving service. The verse displays liberty not to be abused but used in loving service.

The witnesses agree: the Galatians "have been called unto liberty" — freedom from the law's yoke (Barnes, Robertson); but liberty is not to be made "an occasion (pretext, base) to the flesh," not license to sin or excuse for fleshly indulgence (Barnes, Calvin); rather "by love serve (be slaves to) one another," the free making themselves servants of each other through love (Henry, Wesley). The verse displays liberty used in love. The verse marks the calling: unto liberty. The verse displays the abuse: occasion to the flesh. The verse displays the right use: serve in love. The verse displays the paradox: free yet servants. The liberty displays freedom expressing itself in loving service. The verse displays the liberty: called. The verse displays the flesh: not indulged. The verse displays the love: serving. Liberty not to be abused but used in loving service displays the right use of Christian freedom: the Galatians were called to liberty, but it is not license for the flesh — rather, the free are by love to make themselves servants of one another, freedom fulfilling itself in mutual loving service. The verse displays the law of love, in which the whole law is fulfilled (v.14).
14:
"For all the law is fulfilled in one word, even in this; Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself." The witnesses note the whole law fulfilled in love of neighbour. On "all the law is fulfilled in one word," the grammatical voices note the summary. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares that "all the law is fulfilled (summed up and accomplished) in one word (one saying, one precept)" — the whole of the law's requirement toward others is gathered into a single command.

On "Thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself," the witnesses note the command. Barnes and Calvin note the citation (Leviticus 19:18, as Christ taught): "thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself" — love is the fulfilling of the law; he who truly loves his neighbour does all that the law requires toward him.

The lutheran Luther notes the connection to liberty: the freedom from the law as a means of justification does not abolish love; rather, the free man, serving by love (v.13), fulfils the very law's intent — love does what law commanded. The verse displays the whole law fulfilled in love of neighbour.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declares "all the law is fulfilled (summed up and accomplished) in one word (one precept)" — the law's requirement toward others gathered into one command (Barnes, Robertson); namely "thou shalt love thy neighbour as thyself" (Leviticus 19:18), love being the fulfilling of the law, the one who loves doing all the law requires (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the free man, serving by love, fulfils the law's intent. The verse displays love fulfilling the law. The verse marks the summary: all in one word. The verse displays the command: love thy neighbour. The verse displays the fulfilment: love does the law. The verse displays the connection: liberty and love. The summary displays the whole law gathered into love. The verse displays the law: fulfilled. The verse displays the word: love. The verse displays the neighbour: loved as self. The whole law fulfilled in love of neighbour displays the unity of God's commandment toward others: the entire law is summed up and accomplished in the one precept, "love thy neighbour as thyself" — so that the believer, free from the law as a means of justification, fulfils its true intent by love. The verse displays the law of love, set against mutual destruction (v.15).
15:
"But if ye bite and devour one another, take heed that ye be not consumed one of another." The brief verse notes the warning against mutual destruction. The grammatical voices note the figure. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's vivid warning, the dark opposite of loving service: "if ye bite and devour one another" — the figure of wild beasts tearing each other — picturing the strife, contention, and mutual injury that the Galatians' disputes were producing.

On "consumed one of another," the witnesses note the outcome. Barnes and Calvin note the dire result: "take heed that ye be not consumed (destroyed, eaten up) one of another" — such mutual biting will end in the ruin of all; contention devours a church as beasts devour one another.

The lutheran Luther notes the application: the legalistic controversies had bred bitter strife; Paul warns that this party spirit, if unchecked, will destroy the whole community. The verse displays the warning against mutual destruction.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's vivid warning — "if ye bite and devour one another" (the figure of wild beasts tearing each other), picturing the strife and mutual injury of their disputes (Barnes, Robertson); the dire result, "take heed that ye be not consumed (destroyed) one of another," contention ending in the ruin of all (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the party spirit, unchecked, will destroy the community. The verse displays the warning against strife. The verse marks the figure: bite and devour. The verse displays the outcome: consumed. The verse displays the strife: mutual injury. The verse displays the ruin: of all. The warning displays contention devouring the church. The verse displays the biting: of beasts. The verse displays the devouring: mutual. The verse displays the consuming: of all. The warning against mutual destruction displays the dark opposite of loving service: if the Galatians bite and devour one another in strife — like wild beasts — they will end by consuming each other, contention devouring the whole church. The verse displays the ruin of strife, escaped by walking in the Spirit (v.16).
16:
"This I say then, Walk in the Spirit, and ye shall not fulfil the lust of the flesh." The witnesses note the remedy: walk in the Spirit and not fulfil the flesh. On "walk in the Spirit," the grammatical voices note the command. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's positive direction: "walk in (by, according to) the Spirit" — order the whole conduct of life by the guidance and power of the Holy Spirit; the Spirit-led walk is the remedy against the flesh.

On "ye shall not fulfil the lust of the flesh," the witnesses note the promise. Barnes and Calvin note the assured result: "ye shall not fulfil (carry out, gratify) the lust (desire, craving) of the flesh" — walking by the Spirit, the believer will not give in to the cravings of his corrupt nature; the Spirit overcomes the flesh.

The puritan Henry and lutheran Luther note the dynamic: the flesh is not eradicated but overcome; the way to defeat its lusts is not the law but the indwelling Spirit, by whose power the believer walks in holiness. The verse displays the remedy: walk in the Spirit and not fulfil the flesh.

The witnesses agree: Paul's positive direction "walk in (by, according to) the Spirit" — ordering the whole conduct of life by the Holy Spirit's guidance and power, the remedy against the flesh (Barnes, Robertson); the assured result "ye shall not fulfil (gratify) the lust (craving) of the flesh," the believer not giving in to his corrupt nature, the Spirit overcoming the flesh (Barnes, Calvin); the flesh overcome not by the law but by the indwelling Spirit (Henry, Luther). The verse displays walking in the Spirit. The verse marks the command: walk in the Spirit. The verse displays the promise: not fulfil the flesh. The verse displays the power: the Spirit overcomes. The verse displays the means: not the law. The remedy displays the Spirit-led walk defeating the flesh. The verse displays the walk: by the Spirit. The verse displays the lust: not fulfilled. The verse displays the victory: the Spirit's. The remedy — walk in the Spirit and not fulfil the flesh — displays the true power for holiness: ordering one's whole life by the Holy Spirit's guidance, the believer will not gratify the cravings of his corrupt nature, for the Spirit overcomes the flesh where the law could not. The verse displays the Spirit-led walk, grounded in the warfare of flesh and Spirit (v.17).
17:
"For the flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh: and these are contrary the one to the other: so that ye cannot do the things that ye would." The witnesses note the warfare of flesh and Spirit in the believer. On "the flesh lusteth against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh," the grammatical voices note the conflict. Barnes and Robertson note Paul describes the internal warfare: "the flesh (the corrupt nature) lusteth (wars, strives with desire) against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh" — two opposed principles in conflict within the believer.

On "these are contrary the one to the other," the witnesses note the antagonism. Barnes and Calvin note that flesh and Spirit are "contrary (opposed, set against) one another" — their aims and desires are wholly antagonistic; there is no peace between them in this life.

On "so that ye cannot do the things that ye would," the interpreters note the effect. Barnes and Calvin note that because of this conflict, the believer "cannot do the things that ye would" — the flesh hinders the Spirit's good desires, and the Spirit checks the flesh's evil ones, so that neither has free, unhindered course; the believer is kept from both the full evil the flesh would do and (in this life) the full good the Spirit would do. The verse displays the warfare of flesh and Spirit in the believer.

The witnesses agree: Paul describes the internal warfare — "the flesh (corrupt nature) lusteth (wars) against the Spirit, and the Spirit against the flesh," two opposed principles in conflict within the believer (Barnes, Robertson); "contrary one to another," their aims wholly antagonistic, no peace between them in this life (Barnes, Calvin); "so that ye cannot do the things ye would," each checking the other so neither has free course — the Spirit restraining the flesh's evil, the flesh hindering the Spirit's good (Barnes, Calvin). The verse displays the inner warfare. The verse marks the conflict: flesh against Spirit. The verse displays the antagonism: contrary. The verse displays the effect: cannot do as you would. The verse displays the restraint: mutual checking. The warfare displays the two natures striving in the believer. The verse displays the flesh: warring. The verse displays the Spirit: opposing. The verse displays the conflict: within. The warfare of flesh and Spirit in the believer displays the lifelong inner conflict of the Christian: the corrupt nature and the indwelling Spirit war against each other, wholly contrary, so that the believer cannot freely do all he would — the Spirit checking the flesh's evil and the flesh hindering the Spirit's good. The verse displays the inward struggle, resolved by the Spirit's leading (v.18).
18:
"But if ye be led of the Spirit, ye are not under the law." The brief verse notes that those led by the Spirit are not under the law. The grammatical voices note the freedom. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's conclusion: "if ye be led of (by) the Spirit (governed and guided by the Holy Spirit), ye are not under the law" — those whom the Spirit leads are free from the law's bondage and condemnation.

On "not under the law," the witnesses note the deliverance. Barnes and Calvin note that being "under the law" means under its yoke, its curse, and its condemning power; the Spirit-led believer is delivered from this, walking in the freedom of grace, the law's righteous requirement being fulfilled in him by the Spirit.

The lutheran Luther notes the assurance: the believer led by the Spirit need not fear the law, for he is no longer under it as a slave but walks in the liberty of the gospel; the Spirit's leading and the law's bondage are mutually exclusive. The verse displays that those led by the Spirit are not under the law.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concludes "if ye be led of (by) the Spirit, ye are not under the law," those guided by the Holy Spirit being free from the law's bondage (Barnes, Robertson); "under the law" meaning under its yoke, curse, and condemning power, from which the Spirit-led believer is delivered into the freedom of grace (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the believer led by the Spirit need not fear the law. The verse displays freedom from the law. The verse marks the leading: of the Spirit. The verse displays the freedom: not under the law. The verse displays the deliverance: from curse and yoke. The verse displays the exclusivity: Spirit or law. The freedom displays the Spirit-led believer delivered from the law. The verse displays the leading: by the Spirit. The verse displays the law: not over them. The verse displays the liberty: of grace. That those led by the Spirit are not under the law displays the believer's deliverance: whoever is governed and guided by the Holy Spirit is free from the law's yoke, curse, and condemnation, walking in the liberty of grace — for the Spirit's leading and the law's bondage cannot coexist. The verse displays the freedom of the Spirit-led, set against the works of the flesh (vv.19-21).
19:
"Now the works of the flesh are manifest, which are these; Adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lasciviousness," The brief verse notes the works of the flesh, beginning with the sins of impurity. The grammatical voices note the catalogue. Barnes and Robertson note Paul lists "the works of the flesh" — the deeds the corrupt nature produces — which are "manifest" (open, evident, plainly recognizable as evil); he begins with the sins of sensuality: "adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lasciviousness."

On these first sins, the witnesses note the impurity. Barnes and Calvin note the four sins of the flesh in the narrower sense: adultery and fornication (sexual sins), uncleanness (impurity of every kind), and lasciviousness (wantonness, shameless indulgence) — the gross works of corrupt desire.

The lutheran Luther notes the purpose: Paul sets the dark catalogue of the flesh's works over against the fruit of the Spirit (v.22), that believers may know and shun what the flesh produces. The verse displays the works of the flesh, beginning with the sins of impurity.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul lists "the works of the flesh" — the deeds the corrupt nature produces, "manifest" (open, plainly evil) — beginning with the sins of sensuality, "adultery, fornication, uncleanness, lasciviousness" (Barnes, Robertson); the four sins of impurity — sexual sin, uncleanness of every kind, shameless wantonness (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes Paul sets the flesh's works against the Spirit's fruit, that believers may shun them. The verse displays the works of the flesh. The verse marks the catalogue: works of the flesh. The verse displays the character: manifest, evil. The verse displays the sins: impurity. The verse displays the purpose: to shun them. The catalogue displays the corrupt nature's manifest works. The verse displays the flesh: its works. The verse displays the impurity: listed. The verse displays the manifestness: evident. The works of the flesh, beginning with the sins of impurity, display what the corrupt nature openly produces: adultery, fornication, uncleanness, and lasciviousness — the gross sins of sensual desire, set forth plainly that believers may know and shun the deeds of the flesh. The verse displays the catalogue of the flesh's works, continued in the sins of false worship and strife (vv.20-21).
20:
"Idolatry, witchcraft, hatred, variance, emulations, wrath, strife, seditions, heresies," The brief verse notes the works of the flesh in false worship and contention. The grammatical voices note the continuation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul continues the catalogue: "idolatry (false worship, the worship of idols) and witchcraft (sorcery, pharmakeia, dealing with evil powers and drugs)" — the sins against true religion — followed by the sins of contention.

On the sins of strife, the witnesses note the list. Barnes and Ellicott note the works of discord: "hatred, variance (strife, quarrels), emulations (jealousy), wrath (outbursts of anger), strife (factions, self-seeking), seditions (divisions, dissensions), heresies (parties, sects)" — the social sins that tear apart fellowship.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the breadth: the flesh produces not only fleshly lusts but sins of the temper and spirit — pride, anger, party-spirit — which are no less its works than sensuality. The verse displays the works of the flesh in false worship and contention.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul continues the catalogue — "idolatry (false worship) and witchcraft (sorcery, pharmakeia)," sins against true religion (Barnes, Robertson); then the works of discord — "hatred, variance (quarrels), emulations (jealousy), wrath, strife (factions), seditions (dissensions), heresies (parties)," the sins that tear apart fellowship (Barnes, Ellicott). Wesley notes the flesh produces sins of temper and spirit, not only sensuality. The verse displays false worship and strife. The verse marks the worship: idolatry, witchcraft. The verse displays the contention: hatred, strife, divisions. The verse displays the breadth: temper and spirit. The verse displays the discord: tearing fellowship. The catalogue displays the flesh's sins against religion and against others. The verse displays the idolatry: false worship. The verse displays the strife: manifold. The verse displays the divisions: party-spirit. The works of the flesh in false worship and contention display the further reach of the corrupt nature: idolatry and witchcraft against true religion, and a long train of social sins — hatred, jealousy, wrath, faction, dissension, and party-spirit — that tear apart the fellowship of the church. The verse displays the divisive works of the flesh, closed with a solemn warning (v.21).
21:
"Envyings, murders, drunkenness, revellings, and such like: of the which I tell you before, as I have also told you in time past, that they which do such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God." The witnesses note the warning that the doers of the flesh's works shall not inherit the kingdom. On the close of the catalogue, the grammatical voices note the final sins. Barnes and Robertson note the list ends with "envyings, murders, drunkenness, revellings (carousing, riotous feasting), and such like" — the catalogue not exhaustive but representative of the flesh's works.

On "I tell you before... that they which do such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God," the witnesses note the solemn warning. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's earnest forewarning, repeated as before: "they which do (practise, habitually) such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God" — those whose lives are characterized by these works of the flesh are excluded from the kingdom.

The eastern Cyril and lutheran Luther note the seriousness: this is no idle threat; the habitual practice of the flesh's works is incompatible with saving faith and bars from the inheritance — a sobering warning even to professing believers. The verse displays the warning that the doers of the flesh's works shall not inherit the kingdom.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catalogue ends with "envyings, murders, drunkenness, revellings (carousing), and such like," representative not exhaustive (Barnes, Robertson); Paul's solemn forewarning, repeated as before, "they which do (habitually practise) such things shall not inherit the kingdom of God," those whose lives are marked by these works excluded from the kingdom (Barnes, Calvin); no idle threat, the habitual practice incompatible with saving faith (Cyril, Luther). The verse displays the warning. The verse marks the close: envyings, drunkenness, revellings. The verse displays the warning: shall not inherit. The verse displays the practice: habitual. The verse displays the seriousness: a true bar. The warning displays the doers of the flesh excluded from the kingdom. The verse displays the works: closing. The verse displays the warning: solemn. The verse displays the kingdom: forfeited. The warning that the doers of the flesh's works shall not inherit the kingdom displays the gravity of the catalogue: Paul forewarns, as he had before, that those whose lives are habitually marked by these works of the flesh — sensuality, idolatry, strife, drunkenness, and the like — are shut out from the kingdom of God. The verse displays the exclusion of the fleshly, set against the fruit of the Spirit (vv.22-23).
22:
"But the fruit of the Spirit is love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith," The witnesses note the fruit of the Spirit, beginning with love, joy, and peace. On "the fruit of the Spirit," the grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note the deliberate contrast with "the works of the flesh": the Spirit's product is called "fruit" (singular — one organic cluster, the natural growth of the Spirit's life) not "works"; it is what the indwelling Spirit produces in the believer.

On the graces named, the witnesses note the cluster. Barnes and Calvin note the nine graces, beginning with "love (the root of all), joy, peace (toward God and man), longsuffering (patience under wrong), gentleness (kindness), goodness (benevolence), faith (fidelity, faithfulness)" — the beautiful character the Spirit forms.

The eastern Cyril and lutheran Luther note the source: these graces are not the achievements of human effort or the law but the fruit of the Holy Spirit dwelling in the heart; they grow where the Spirit reigns. The verse displays the fruit of the Spirit, beginning with love, joy, and peace.

The witnesses agree: the deliberate contrast with the flesh's works — the Spirit's product is "fruit" (singular, one organic cluster, natural growth) not "works," what the indwelling Spirit produces (Barnes, Robertson); the graces beginning with "love (the root), joy, peace, longsuffering (patience), gentleness (kindness), goodness (benevolence), faith (faithfulness)" — the character the Spirit forms (Barnes, Calvin); these the fruit of the Spirit, not human effort or the law (Cyril, Luther). The verse displays the fruit of the Spirit. The verse marks the contrast: fruit not works. The verse displays the graces: love, joy, peace. The verse displays the cluster: organic. The verse displays the source: the Spirit. The fruit displays the Spirit's character formed in the believer. The verse displays the love: the root. The verse displays the joy: and peace. The verse displays the Spirit: its fruit. The fruit of the Spirit, beginning with love, joy, and peace, displays what the indwelling Spirit produces, over against the works of the flesh: not the achievements of effort or law, but one organic cluster of graces — love, joy, peace, longsuffering, gentleness, goodness, faith — the beautiful character the Spirit forms where he reigns. The verse displays the Spirit's fruit, completed and freed from the law (v.23).
23:
"Meekness, temperance: against such there is no law." The brief verse notes the fruit completed and free from the law. The grammatical voices note the last graces. Barnes and Robertson note the catalogue of the Spirit's fruit closes with "meekness (gentleness of spirit, humility) and temperance (self-control, mastery of the appetites)" — completing the nine graces.

On "against such there is no law," the witnesses note the freedom. Barnes and Calvin note the striking conclusion: "against such there is no law" — no law condemns these graces; the law has nothing to say against love, joy, peace, and the rest, for they are the very things the law aimed at; the Spirit's fruit fulfils, and stands above, the law.

The lutheran Luther notes the point: the believer who bears the Spirit's fruit needs no law to compel or condemn him, for he does freely and from the heart what the law could only command; the law is not against, but for, such conduct. The verse displays the fruit completed and free from the law.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catalogue closes with "meekness (gentleness, humility) and temperance (self-control)," completing the nine graces (Barnes, Robertson); the striking conclusion "against such there is no law" — no law condemns these graces, for they are what the law aimed at, the Spirit's fruit fulfilling and standing above the law (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the believer who bears the fruit needs no law to compel or condemn him. The verse displays the fruit free from law. The verse marks the close: meekness, temperance. The verse displays the freedom: no law against them. The verse displays the fulfilment: the law's aim. The verse displays the point: needs no compelling. The fruit displays the Spirit's graces standing above the law. The verse displays the meekness: and temperance. The verse displays the law: not against. The verse displays the freedom: of the fruit. The fruit completed and free from the law displays the crown of the Spirit's work: the graces close with meekness and temperance, and against all such there is no law — for the Spirit's fruit is the very thing the law aimed at, fulfilling it from the heart and standing above its compulsion and condemnation. The verse displays the law-transcending fruit, borne by those who crucified the flesh (v.24).
24:
"And they that are Christ's have crucified the flesh with the affections and lusts." The brief verse notes that Christ's people have crucified the flesh. The grammatical voices note the act. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's declaration: "they that are Christ's (those who truly belong to him) have crucified (put to death, as on a cross) the flesh with the affections (passions) and lusts (desires)" — believers have decisively renounced and put to death the corrupt nature.

On "crucified the flesh," the witnesses note the manner. Barnes and Calvin note the figure of crucifixion: as Christ was crucified, so the believer, united to him, has crucified the flesh — a decisive, deliberate putting to death of the sinful nature with its passions, begun in conversion and continued through life.

The lutheran Luther notes the mark: this is the distinguishing mark of those who are Christ's — not perfection, but the daily crucifying of the flesh; the believer wars against and mortifies his corrupt desires. The verse displays that Christ's people have crucified the flesh.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul declares "they that are Christ's (who truly belong to him) have crucified (put to death) the flesh with the affections (passions) and lusts (desires)," decisively renouncing the corrupt nature (Barnes, Robertson); the figure of crucifixion — as Christ was crucified, so the believer, united to him, puts the flesh to death, begun in conversion and continued through life (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes this is the mark of Christ's people — the daily crucifying of the flesh. The verse displays the flesh crucified. The verse marks the people: Christ's. The verse displays the act: crucified the flesh. The verse displays the object: affections and lusts. The verse displays the mark: of believers. The act displays Christ's people putting the flesh to death. The verse displays the flesh: crucified. The verse displays the passions: mortified. The verse displays the union: with Christ. That Christ's people have crucified the flesh displays the mark of those who truly belong to him: united to the crucified Christ, they have decisively put to death the corrupt nature with its passions and desires — a crucifixion begun in conversion and continued in the daily mortifying of sin. The verse displays the crucified flesh of believers, who therefore walk in the Spirit (v.25).
25:
"If we live in the Spirit, let us also walk in the Spirit." The brief verse notes the call to walk by the Spirit by whom we live. The grammatical voices note the inference. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's logic: "if we live in (by) the Spirit (if the Spirit is the source of our spiritual life) let us also walk in (by) the Spirit" — our daily conduct should match the source of our life; profession must issue in practice.

On "live... walk," the witnesses note the consistency. Barnes and Calvin note the distinction: to "live" by the Spirit is to have spiritual life from him (the inward principle); to "walk" by the Spirit is to order one's outward conduct by him (the practical course) — the two must agree.

The lutheran Luther notes the exhortation: those who have received the Spirit's life are to make it visible in a Spirit-led walk; the indicative (we live by the Spirit) grounds the imperative (let us walk by the Spirit). The verse displays the call to walk by the Spirit by whom we live.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's logic — "if we live in (by) the Spirit (the source of our spiritual life), let us also walk in (by) the Spirit," our conduct matching the source of our life (Barnes, Robertson); the distinction between living by the Spirit (the inward principle of life) and walking by the Spirit (the outward course), which must agree (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes the indicative grounds the imperative. The verse displays living and walking by the Spirit. The verse marks the life: by the Spirit. The verse displays the walk: by the Spirit. The verse displays the consistency: profession and practice. The verse displays the ground: the Spirit's life. The call displays the Spirit's life issuing in a Spirit-led walk. The verse displays the living: in the Spirit. The verse displays the walking: in the Spirit. The verse displays the agreement: of both. The call to walk by the Spirit by whom we live displays the consistency the gospel requires: if the Spirit is the source of the believer's life, then his daily conduct must be ordered by the same Spirit — the inward life issuing in an outward walk that matches it. The verse displays the Spirit-led life and walk, against vainglory (v.26).
26:
"Let us not be desirous of vain glory, provoking one another, envying one another." The brief verse notes the warning against vainglory, provocation, and envy. The grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's closing exhortation of the chapter: "let us not be desirous of vain glory (kenodoxoi — empty, conceited glory-seeking, craving the praise of men)" — the root of much strife.

On "provoking... envying one another," the witnesses note the twin fruits of vainglory. Barnes and Calvin note the two consequences: "provoking (challenging, irritating) one another" — the stronger inciting the weaker — and "envying one another" — the weaker resenting the stronger; both spring from the empty pursuit of glory.

The lutheran Luther notes the application: vainglory was at the root of the Galatians' divisions and the false teachers' ambition; the cure for the strife that devours a church is the renouncing of empty self-seeking. The verse displays the warning against vainglory, provocation, and envy.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's closing charge — "let us not be desirous of vain glory (empty, conceited glory-seeking, craving men's praise)," the root of much strife (Barnes, Robertson); its twin fruits, "provoking (challenging, irritating) one another" and "envying one another," the stronger inciting and the weaker resenting, both from empty glory-seeking (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes vainglory was at the root of the Galatians' divisions. The verse displays the warning against vainglory. The verse marks the root: vain glory. The verse displays the fruits: provoking, envying. The verse displays the strife: from self-seeking. The verse displays the cure: renounce empty glory. The warning displays vainglory breeding provocation and envy. The verse displays the glory: empty. The verse displays the provoking: of one another. The verse displays the envying: mutual. The warning against vainglory, provocation, and envy displays the root of the church's strife: the empty craving for the praise of men breeds both the provoking of others and the envying of them — and the cure for the contention that devours a fellowship is the renouncing of self-seeking glory. The verse displays the peril of vainglory, leading to the gentle restoring of the fallen (ch. 6).
